dateStatements.label: Composition or translation
dateStatements.value: Probably around 1400 or in the early fifteenth century

entities.description: An anonymous Middle English verse translation of the Joseph and Aseneth narrative, probably composed around 1400 or in the early fifteenth century. Its 32-line prologue says that a lady requested a Latin-to-English translation. The narrative is predominantly in rhyme royal and survives uniquely in Huntington mssEL 26 A 13. A 49-line personal lament follows in the same witness and is associated with the poem, but whether it was originally its epilogue remains disputed.
entities.label: The Storie of Asneth
notes: No medieval author is named. John Walton has been proposed on circumstantial grounds, but the attribution is not accepted here as known authorship.
West Midland is retained as a dialect description, not silently converted into a proven place of composition.

rights.basis: The anonymous medieval work and fifteenth-century witness are centuries outside copyright term. Modern editions, photographs, and markup are separately scoped.
rights.component: Medieval Middle English literary content, including the associated medieval lament

bibliographic.citation: Henry Noble MacCracken, 'The Storie of Asneth: An Unknown Middle English Translation of a Lost Latin Version,' Journal of English and Germanic Philology 9, no. 2 (1910): 224–264.

bibliographic.title: The Storie of Asneth: An Unknown Middle English Translation of a Lost Latin Version

entities.description: The first edition of the unique Middle English poem, published with an English introduction and commentary and Latin comparison excerpts from Vincent of Beauvais. MacCracken labels the final seven-stanza lament as an epilogue and describes his insertions and footnotes.
entities.label: Henry Noble MacCracken, The Storie of Asneth (1910)

entities.description: Vincent of Beauvais's thirteenth-century abridged Latin treatment of Joseph and Aseneth. MacCracken printed chapters VI.118–124 beneath the English text as a comparison, while recognizing that omitted material required a fuller Latin source for the English translation.
entities.label: Vincent of Beauvais, Speculum historiale VI.118–124

entities.description: A fuller medieval Latin version of Joseph and Aseneth that supplies material omitted from Vincent's abridgment and is identified by later scholarship as the textual source tradition translated by the Middle English poet. Extant witnesses include CCCC MSS 424 and 288; the English translator's own exemplar is not identified with either codex.
entities.label: Liber de Aseneth / Historia Assenech (fuller Latin recension)

rights.basis: The medieval Latin and earlier Greek literary content are centuries outside copyright term; modern editions and photographs are separate components.
rights.component: Medieval Latin work and its ancient source tradition

entities.description: The earlier Greek narrative tradition about Joseph's marriage to Aseneth, her conversion, and the attack by Pharaoh's son. The fuller Latin recension is a version in this wider transmission; no surviving Greek manuscript is asserted as the direct physical source of the Middle English translation.
entities.label: Joseph and Aseneth

entities.description: A twelfth-century Latin manuscript preserving Liber de Aseneth on ff.42r–59v. M. R. James treated it as the oldest copy known to him and collated it with CCCC MS 288.

next_steps: Consult Dwyer's full textual argument and subsequent Joseph and Aseneth Latin-recension scholarship.
Collate distinctive English readings against CCCC MSS 424 and 288 and other Latin witnesses.
Keep the translation relation at abstract-work level unless a physical exemplar is demonstrated.
openQuestions.question: Which physical Latin manuscript or textual subfamily supplied the anonymous Middle English translator's exemplar?

next_steps: Consult Hume's full article and Peck's textual discussion alongside Brown and Robbins's separate treatment.
Compare hand, ruling, spacing, decoration, and textual transitions on ff.126v–127r at full available resolution.
Retain both MacCracken's epilogue association and DIMEV's separate work record in the catalog model.
openQuestions.question: Was the 49-line personal lament originally composed as an epilogue to the Storie, attached in an exemplar, or merely copied after it?

next_steps: Assess the proposed John Walton and patronage hypotheses against current peer-reviewed scholarship.
Distinguish translator dialect, scribal dialect, and manuscript production before assigning a composition place.
Retain MacCracken's Warwickshire suggestion as historical speculation rather than a normalized place.
openQuestions.question: Can the anonymous translator, exact composition date, or place be established more securely than an early-fifteenth-century West Midland linguistic association?

next_steps: Ask the Huntington whether photographs from the Ellesmere / Bridgewater Library survive in institutional files.
Search MacCracken archival papers and JEGP editorial correspondence.
If located, compare them with the current color facsimile to assess any lost edge or binding evidence.
openQuestions.question: Do the pre-1910 manuscript photographs used by MacCracken survive, and if so where and under what access terms?

next_steps: Audit the XML HI spans against ff.116r–127r at the best Huntington resolution available.
Record segment-level categories without assuming that every italic sequence has one function.
Preserve the typography until a fully evidenced normalization is available.
openQuestions.question: Can every italic span in MacCracken's Middle English transcription be assigned a precise function such as abbreviation expansion, supplied reading, or another editorial signal?

reviewNotes: The intellectual subject of the paired metadata manifest is the anonymous Middle English Storie of Asneth, with the final 49-line lament retained as an associated and disputed boundary, not the whole 1910 article.
MacCracken's 'Ellesmere MS. No. 4' is the former Bridgewater Library designation of current Huntington mssEL 26 A 13; current foliation ff.116r–127r corresponds to his ff.121a–132a.

MacCracken explicitly says that he copied his text from photographs arranged with the Earl of Ellesmere's permission. The historical photographs are therefore modeled between the edition and manuscript; their present location is unknown.

The Latin Speculum historiale text printed by MacCracken is editorial comparison material, not the Middle English translator's physical exemplar and not an intrinsic second language of the cataloged medieval work.

MacCracken's claim that no copy of the fuller Historia Assenech was known to him records the state of knowledge in 1910. Peck's inspected METS discussion reports Dwyer's later identification of the surviving fuller Latin text, but none of its witnesses is asserted to be the translator's actual copy.
The complete Huntington and Parker codices are cached in ignored ZIP bundles. Work locators point into those complete objects rather than replacing them with cropped extracts.

Huntington's aggregate contributor heading includes John Walton among authors represented elsewhere in the codex. It is not component-level evidence that Walton wrote the Storie; current work-level sources call the poem anonymous.

The DIMEV core record describes a conventional 933-line extent while ending at the Storie's own explicit on f.126v, and cross-refers to a separate record for the seven-stanza lament on ff.126v–127r. That tension is preserved rather than normalized away.

lineage.summary: The checked-in XML is a repository copy of University of Michigan item CME00110, an encoding of Henry Noble MacCracken's 1910 JEGP article and edition. MacCracken transcribed the unique Middle English witness from photographs of what he called Ellesmere MS No. 4, now The Huntington Library, mssEL 26 A 13, current ff. 116r–127r (his ff. 121a–132a). His article prints the Middle English poem and an immediately following 49-line lament as an 'Epilogue,' with corresponding excerpts from Vincent of Beauvais's Speculum historiale VI.118–124 at the foot of the printed pages. The U-M encoding serializes those parallel English and Latin streams. Later scholarship identifies the English poem as a translation of a fuller Latin Liber de Aseneth / Historia Assenech recension represented by Corpus Christi College, Cambridge MSS 424 and 288; the translator's actual physical Latin exemplar remains unidentified. The lament's status as an original epilogue is disputed and is separately indexed by DIMEV.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous Middle English translator
catalogSummary.date: Probably around 1400 or early fifteenth century; unique surviving witness approximately 1450; first edition 1910
catalogSummary.region: England; West Midland dialect association; exact place of composition unknown
catalogSummary.title: The Storie of Asneth

catalogingSubject.label: The Storie of Asneth
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloged subject is the anonymous Middle English verse translation conventionally called The Storie of Asneth: its 32-line prologue and 851-line narrative preserved uniquely in Huntington mssEL 26 A 13, current ff.116r–126v. A 49-line personal lament immediately follows on ff.126v–127r and is retained as an associated, disputed boundary because MacCracken printed it as an '[Epilogue]' while DIMEV indexes it separately and Peck judges that it was probably not originally part of the Storie. The subject excludes the physical codex, MacCracken's modern introduction and closing note, the Latin Speculum historiale comparison, the 1910 edition as an editorial object, the University of Michigan encoding, and the checked-in XML; those layers remain separately modeled in lineage and content notes.

contentParts.label: CME00110 encoded article and edition
notes: This root accounts for the source item's editorial and comparison material; only the medieval English parts define the abstract cataloging subject.
contentParts.summary: The full source item represented by the XML: modern introduction, Middle English prologue and narrative, associated lament, Latin comparison, and final commentary.

contentParts.label: MacCracken's modern English introduction
notes: This modern prose is source evidence and editorial context, not part of the medieval work's form or language facets.
contentParts.summary: Editorial overview of the story, style, supposed author and region, manuscript, photographs, transcription practice, Greek and translated traditions, and the then-unidentified fuller Latin source.

contentParts.label: Prologue to The Storie of Asneth
notes: DIMEV describes eight four-line stanzas with abab rhyme. The XML has six LG groups whose uneven sizes reflect encoding rather than the intrinsic eight-quatrain form.
contentParts.summary: The poet describes a lady's request for an English translation, protests age and limited skill, accepts the commission out of obedience, asks God to guide the work, and asks his mistress to gloss any faults.

contentParts.label: The Storie of Asneth narrative

locators.value: pp.227–262, lines 33–883; two additional asterisk rows mark omitted material

notes: The XML has 853 L elements in this division, but two contain only '* * * * *'; 851 are verse lines.
The poem explicitly closes before the lament.
contentParts.summary: The verse narrative from Pharaoh sending Joseph through Egypt, through Aseneth's conversion, angelic vision, honeycomb miracle, marriage, and attempted abduction, to Joseph's rule and the poem's explicit.

contentParts.label: Associated lament, printed by MacCracken as '[Epilogue]'
notes: Unit type 'epilogue' records its source-edition presentation, not a settled claim that it was originally part of the Storie.
DIMEV record 27 indexes it independently under IMEV/NIMEV 2; DIMEV record 623 cross-refers to it as a text considered an epilogue.
contentParts.summary: Seven rhyme-royal stanzas condemning death, mourning a young gentlewoman identified as the speaker's mistress, praying for her soul, and praising her virtues.

contentParts.label: Vincent of Beauvais Latin comparison
notes: This Latin is editorial comparison material, not an intrinsic part or second language of the Middle English work and not the translator's exact physical exemplar.
contentParts.summary: MacCracken's excerpt of Speculum historiale VI.118–124, printed at the foot of the English pages and serialized after the Middle English in CME00110.

contentParts.label: MacCracken's closing note
notes: This is modern editorial commentary, not part of the medieval poem.
contentParts.summary: A short note pointing to Lydgate's reference to Aseneth in To Mary the Queen of Heaven and stating that MacCracken had found no other references in English literature.

assertion.notes: The normalized range represents uncertainty around c.1400 / early-fifteenth-century scholarly dating; it does not date the surviving manuscript.

dateStatements.display: Probably around 1400 or in the early fifteenth century

assertion.notes: The current catalog's searchable date range is 1430–1470 and display date approximately 1450.

dateStatements.display: 1910 in Henry Noble MacCracken's JEGP edition

extent.description: The medieval verse unit represented here has a 32-line prologue and 851-line Storie narrative (883 core lines total), followed by a 49-line associated lament, for 932 actual verse lines. MacCracken's lineation reaches 933 because number 884 is absent between the core explicit at 883 and lament opening at 885. The XML has 934 L elements because two additional L elements contain only rows of asterisks marking omitted material. Its 134 LG elements reflect source-edition / encoding grouping and should not be treated as a clean manuscript stanza count. The complete encoded article additionally contains eight modern English introductory P elements, nine Latin comparison P elements, and one closing English commentary P element.

assertion.notes: The XML's LG boundaries are influenced by the source edition and do not cleanly reproduce eight four-line prologue stanzas, so line and rhyme-form evidence governs.

formStatements.description: The Storie is verse: a 32-line prologue in eight quatrains followed by a predominantly rhyme-royal narrative. The associated lament consists of seven rhyme-royal stanzas.

assertion.notes: The prologue explicitly says the poet translates from Latin into English; later scholarship identifies the fuller Latin recension at work level.
DIMEV describes the associated text as a personal elegy on a young gentlewoman.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: Modern English occurs in MacCracken's introduction and notes, and Latin in his Vincent comparison. Those are edition layers and do not make the cataloged medieval work intrinsically multilingual.
The preferred title is editorial and conventional; the manuscript itself is untitled at the work opening.
The abstract work language is Middle English only. Modern English introduction/commentary and Latin comparison belong to the 1910 edition layer.

MacCracken's old foliation ff.121a–132a maps to current ff.116r–127r, one-based complete-bundle images 245–267 and IIIF c244–c266; the preceding c243 / f.115v is not part of the work and c267 / f.127v follows it.
The 932 actual-line count is not a claim that DIMEV's conventional '933 lines' description is erroneous: DIMEV follows the terminal line number while MacCracken's sequence omits 884.

The historical phrase 'lost Latin version' must not be read as a current claim that the fuller Latin recension is lost. Surviving Latin witnesses are known, while the translator's own physical exemplar remains unidentified.

No change to the project research skill was required: the source-layer, aggregate-author-heading, lost-versus-unidentified-exemplar, disputed-boundary, and complete-manuscript-caching cases encountered here are already covered by its existing guidance.

next_steps: Consult Hume's full argument and Peck's textual notes together with Brown and Robbins's separate-work treatment.
Assess layout, hand, decoration, and transition on ff.126v–127r at full available resolution.
Keep the part-scoped elegy facets and separate DIMEV identifier until the boundary is resolved.
openQuestions.question: Was the seven-stanza personal lament originally an epilogue to The Storie of Asneth, attached in an exemplar, or only copied after it?

next_steps: Consult Dwyer's complete analysis and subsequent scholarship on Latin Joseph and Aseneth recensions.
Collate distinctive Middle English readings against CCCC MSS 424 and 288 and other witnesses.
Do not identify either available Parker manuscript as the translator's direct copy without textual proof.
openQuestions.question: Which physical Latin manuscript or textual subfamily supplied the Middle English translator's exemplar?

next_steps: Evaluate proposed John Walton and patronage identifications against current peer-reviewed scholarship.
Distinguish translator dialect, scribal dialect, and manuscript production before assigning a composition place.
Retain the West Midland characterization and broad date as supported but uncertain.
openQuestions.question: Can the anonymous translator, commissioning lady, exact date, or composition place be identified securely?

next_steps: Audit all HI spans against current ff.116r–127r at the highest Huntington resolution available.
Record segment-level categories and preserve ambiguous typography.
Use MacCracken's footnotes to distinguish exact manuscript readings from silent or typographic editorial interventions.
openQuestions.question: Can every italic span in MacCracken's Middle English text be classified as an abbreviation expansion, editorial supply, uncertain reading, or another function?

assertion.notes: The poem is a Middle English translation preserved in an English manuscript; the exact composition place is unknown.
Peck describes the language as likely West Midland. MacCracken's more specific Warwickshire suggestion remains unconfirmed speculation.
The Middle English poet-translator is anonymous; modern proposals do not establish a known author.
The prologue explicitly describes translation from Latin into English, but no translator is named.

notes: The translator may also be the poet-speaker, but this does not establish personal identity.
The anonymous author of the following lament is not assumed to be the same person.

assertion.notes: The lament is anonymous. Shared authorship with the Storie translator is unresolved.

notes: This is source-edition responsibility, not medieval intellectual authorship.

scope.description: The 1910 article and edition represented by CME00110

assertion.notes: MacCracken says he copied the text from photographs of the manuscript.

subjects.label: Idolatry and renunciation of pagan gods

assertion.notes: The prologue presents a lady requesting the translation. Her identity and the historical circumstances of the commission remain unresolved.

subjects.label: Death, mourning, and praise of a gentlewoman

summaries.value: A lady asks an aging poet to translate Aseneth's story from Latin into English. The narrative tells how Aseneth, proud daughter of the Egyptian priest Potiphar, rejects men and worships idols until she sees Joseph. Joseph refuses to kiss an idolater, prompting her seven-day fast, repentance, and prayer. A heavenly visitor accepts her conversion and performs the miracle of a honeycomb and bees; Joseph and Aseneth marry. Later Pharaoh's son conspires with Dan and Gad to abduct her, but Benjamin, Simeon, and Levi defeat the attack. Aseneth intercedes for the conspirators, and Joseph eventually governs Egypt before returning the crown. The poem closes by naming itself a rude translation and asking the patron's pleasure. A 49-line lament for a young gentlewoman follows in the unique manuscript; MacCracken printed it as an epilogue, but its original attachment remains disputed.

A seven-stanza lament immediately follows the Storie. The speaker denounces cruel death for destroying lovers and noble women, mourns a young mistress, prays that God receive her soul into paradise, and praises her lineage, beauty, courtesy, mercy, and virtue. MacCracken treated it as the Storie's epilogue; DIMEV indexes it independently and Peck considers its original attachment doubtful.

The record catalogs the medieval Middle English translation, not every textual layer serialized in CME00110. MacCracken's English introduction, Vincent of Beauvais's Latin comparison, and closing note are modern edition components. The physical manuscript, historical photographs, edition, digital encoding, and repository XML are linked but not conflated. The lament is retained visibly as an associated disputed component so users can see both MacCracken's continuous presentation and later separate-work cataloging.

assertion.notes: The manuscript has no medieval title rubric for the work; this is the stable editorial and scholarly title.

titles.supplied_by: Henry Noble MacCracken; retained by later scholarship

titles.value: As I on hilly halkes logged me late
Pharao the famus kyng of egipt land aboute

assertion.notes: These lines explicitly close the Storie before the separate lament begins.

titles.value: Þus endeth the storie of asneth to youre remembrance / My rude translacion I pray you tak hit with plesance

assertion.notes: MacCracken instead supplied the bracketed structural label '[Epilogue].'

titles.value: A cruel death contrarious to creatures in kind
Ha, cruell deeth! contrarious to creatures in kynde
Comely, kynde, and curteis, in nobleye of nurture / Vernant in alle vertu, plesaunt and demure

work.author: Anonymous Middle English translator
work.dateDisplay: Probably around 1400 or early fifteenth century; unique surviving witness approximately 1450; first edition 1910

publication.embeddedTitle: THE STORIE OF ASNETH. — AN UNKNOWN MIDDLE ENGLISH TRANSLATION OF A LOST LATIN VERSION.

work.regionDisplay: England; West Midland dialect association; exact place of composition unknown

subjects.label: Idolatry and renunciation of pagan gods
Death, mourning, and praise of a gentlewoman

work.summary: A lady asks an aging poet to translate Aseneth's story from Latin into English. The narrative tells how Aseneth, proud daughter of the Egyptian priest Potiphar, rejects men and worships idols until she sees Joseph. Joseph refuses to kiss an idolater, prompting her seven-day fast, repentance, and prayer. A heavenly visitor accepts her conversion and performs the miracle of a honeycomb and bees; Joseph and Aseneth marry. Later Pharaoh's son conspires with Dan and Gad to abduct her, but Benjamin, Simeon, and Levi defeat the attack. Aseneth intercedes for the conspirators, and Joseph eventually governs Egypt before returning the crown. The poem closes by naming itself a rude translation and asking the patron's pleasure. A 49-line lament for a young gentlewoman follows in the unique manuscript; MacCracken printed it as an epilogue, but its original attachment remains disputed.
